response.evidence: Hispanic or Latino applicants show consistently lower acceptance rates compared to non-Hispanic counterparts across most racial categories. Hispanic Latino acceptance rates: American Indian Female (66.3%), Male (64.8%); Asian Female (77.5%), Male (79.9%); Black Female (69.5%), Male (64.8%); Native Hawaiian Female (50.0%), Male (64.6%); White Female (75.9%), Male (79.2%). Non-Hispanic counterparts show higher rates: 70.8-75.3% for American Indian; 86.5-87.8% for Asian; 73.0-70.5% for Black; 72.7-75.3% for Native Hawaiian; 83.8-84.5% for White. Additionally, Hispanic applicants in most groups have lower average incomes and loan amounts approved, suggesting potential disparate treatment. The pattern is consistent across racial categories, indicating ethnicity may be a factor in approval decisions.

Latino applicants (Hispanic or Latino ethnicity) show consistently lower acceptance rates compared to non-Latino counterparts across multiple demographic groups. Hispanic Latino acceptance rates range from 50.0% to 79.9%, while Non-Hispanic Latino rates range from 70.5% to 87.8%. Specifically: American Indian/Alaska Native Female (66.3% vs 70.8%), American Indian/Alaska Native Male (64.8% vs 75.3%), Asian Female (77.5% vs 86.5%), Asian Male (79.9% vs 87.8%), Black/African American Female (69.5% vs 73.0%), Black/African American Male (64.8% vs 70.5%), Native Hawaiian Male (64.6% vs 75.3%), White Female (75.9% vs 83.8%), White Male (79.2% vs 84.5%). This pattern is systematic across all race/sex combinations, suggesting ethnicity-based disparities in loan approval independent of other measured factors.

Among Hispanic or Latino applicants across all racial categories, acceptance rates are consistently lower than non-Hispanic counterparts in the same race/sex groups. Hispanic Latino groups range from 50.0% to 79.9% acceptance rates, while non-Hispanic counterparts in identical race/sex categories range from 70.5% to 87.8%. Most notably: Asian Female Hispanic (77.5%) vs Non-Hispanic (86.5%); Black Female Hispanic (69.5%) vs Non-Hispanic (73.0%); White Female Hispanic (75.9%) vs Non-Hispanic (83.8%); White Male Hispanic (79.2%) vs Non-Hispanic (84.5%). This pattern is systematic across nearly all demographic combinations, suggesting Hispanic ethnicity is associated with lower loan approval rates independent of race or sex, which constitutes potential discrimination.

Latino applicants show materially lower acceptance rates compared to non-Latino counterparts across most racial groups. Specifically: Asian Hispanic/Latino (79.9%) vs Asian Non-Hispanic (87.8%); Black Hispanic/Latino (64.8%) vs Black Non-Hispanic (70.5%); White Hispanic/Latino (79.2%) vs White Non-Hispanic (84.5%). The pattern is consistent across racial categories, with Latino applicants experiencing 3-8 percentage point lower approval rates. Additionally, Latino applicants across groups tend to have lower average incomes and receive lower average loan amounts, suggesting potential systemic barriers. Only Native Hawaiian groups show near-parity (50% vs 72.7%), but with very small sample sizes. The persistent gap across multiple demographic groups suggests ethnicity-based disparate treatment in loan approval decisions.

Analysis of acceptance rates by race/ethnicity and sex reveals disparities consistent with potential discrimination against Latino applicants. Latino applicants (Hispanic or Latino) show lower acceptance rates across most demographic groups compared to White applicants: Latino females (66.3-77.5%) vs White females (75.9-83.8%), Latino males (64.8-79.9%) vs White males (79.2-84.5%). Most notably, Latino males show the lowest acceptance rates (64.8%) among male groups. Asian applicants show higher acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), while American Indian/Alaska Native applicants also show lower rates (64.8-75.3%). The consistent pattern of lower approval rates for Latino applicants across sex categories, combined with higher average loan amounts and similar debt-to-income ratios to approved groups, suggests potential systemic bias in the approval process against Latino applicants.

Latino applicants (Hispanic or Latino ethnicity) show consistently lower acceptance rates compared to non-Latino counterparts across most demographic groups. Latino acceptance rates range from 50.0% to 79.9%, while non-Latino rates range from 70.5% to 87.8%. Specifically: American Indian/Alaska Native females (Latino: 66.3% vs non-Latino: 70.8%), American Indian/Alaska Native males (Latino: 64.8% vs non-Latino: 75.3%), Asian females (Latino: 77.5% vs non-Latino: 86.5%), Asian males (Latino: 79.9% vs non-Latino: 87.8%), Black females (Latino: 69.5% vs non-Latino: 73.0%), Black males (Latino: 64.8% vs non-Latino: 70.5%), Native Hawaiian females (Latino: 50.0% vs non-Latino: 72.7%), Native Hawaiian males (Latino: 64.6% vs non-Latino: 75.3%), White females (Latino: 75.9% vs non-Latino: 83.8%), and White males (Latino: 79.2% vs non-Latino: 84.5%). This consistent pattern across all racial/ethnic groups and both sexes suggests potential systemic discrimination based on ethnicity in the approval process.

Hispanic or Latino applicants show consistently lower acceptance rates compared to non-Hispanic or Latino applicants across all racial categories in the dataset. Hispanic/Latino acceptance rates range from 50.0% to 79.9%, while non-Hispanic/Latino rates range from 70.5% to 87.8%. For example: American Indian/Alaska Native Female (Hispanic: 66.3% vs Non-Hispanic: 70.8%), Asian Female (Hispanic: 77.5% vs Non-Hispanic: 86.5%), White Male (Hispanic: 79.2% vs Non-Hispanic: 84.5%). Additionally, Hispanic/Latino applicants tend to have lower average incomes and receive lower average loan amounts, which could indicate systemic disparities in lending outcomes not fully explained by standard lending criteria. This pattern is persistent across demographic groups.

Latino applicants (Hispanic or Latino ethnicity) show systematically lower acceptance rates compared to non-Latino applicants across racial groups. Latino acceptance rates range from 50.0% to 79.9%, while non-Latino acceptance rates range from 70.5% to 87.8%. For example: American Indian/Alaska Native Latino females: 66.3% vs non-Latino females: 70.8%; Asian Latino males: 79.9% vs non-Latino males: 87.8%; White Latino males: 79.2% vs non-Latino males: 84.5%; Black/African American Latino males: 64.8% vs non-Latino males: 70.5%. This consistent pattern across multiple demographic groups suggests ethnicity-based disparate treatment in loan approvals, independent of other measured variables like loan amount, debt-to-income ratio, and loan-to-value ratio, which are relatively similar between groups.

Hispanic or Latino applicants across all racial categories show consistently lower acceptance rates compared to non-Hispanic or Latino applicants in the same race/sex groups. Specifically: American Indian Female (66.3% vs 70.8%), American Indian Male (64.8% vs 75.3%), Asian Female (77.5% vs 86.5%), Asian Male (79.9% vs 87.8%), Black Female (69.5% vs 73.0%), Black Male (64.8% vs 70.5%), Native Hawaiian Female (50.0% vs 72.7%), Native Hawaiian Male (64.6% vs 75.3%), White Female (75.9% vs 83.8%), and White Male (79.2% vs 84.5%). This pattern is consistent across all demographic groups examined, suggesting ethnicity-based disparities in loan approval outcomes.

Analysis of acceptance rates by race/ethnicity reveals a clear pattern of disparate impact: Hispanic/Latino applicants across all racial categories show notably lower acceptance rates compared to non-Hispanic counterparts. Specifically: American Indian Hispanic (66.3%) vs non-Hispanic (70.8%), Asian Hispanic (77.5%) vs non-Hispanic (86.5%), Black Hispanic (69.5%) vs non-Hispanic (73.0%), Native Hawaiian Hispanic (50.0%) vs non-Hispanic (72.7%), and White Hispanic (75.9%) vs non-Hispanic (83.8%). The Hispanic applicant groups consistently show 3-23 percentage point lower acceptance rates. This pattern is systemic across all racial categories, suggesting ethnicity-based discrimination in the loan approval process independent of race. While other factors (income, debt-to-income ratios, loan amounts) are similar between groups, the persistent acceptance rate gap associated with Hispanic ethnicity is the key indicator of potential discriminatory treatment.
